## Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

This paper develops a model of irreversible capacity investment that captures the distinctive features of AI infrastructure competition: demand uncertainty with regime switching, duopoly preemption, endogenous default risk, and diminishing returns calibrated to AI scaling laws.
At the center of the model is the training-inference allocation: firms choose how to split capacity between model training (building capabilities for the future) and inference serving (generating revenue today), with the allocation interacting with an endogenous regime switch mechanism in which aggregate training compute accelerates the arrival of transformative AI.
The model produces analytical investment triggers for the single firm and semi-analytical characterization of the duopoly preemption equilibrium with training allocation and default risk (Propositions 1--3), supplemented by numerical findings on the AI investment dilemma and $N$-firm robustness.

Three findings stand out.
First, the training-inference allocation creates a genuine strategic trade-off: firms must balance L-regime inference revenue (needed to survive and service debt) against H-regime training quality (needed to compete in a post-AGI world).
The optimal training fraction $\phi^*$ is increasing in the arrival rate $\lambda$, meaning that more optimistic firms allocate more to training---a prediction that aligns with the cross-sectional variation in observed training intensity across AI labs.

Second, the interaction between training allocation and leverage produces a *faith-based survival* mechanism: higher training investment raises the effective revenue coefficient $A_{\text{eff}}$ through the H-regime continuation value, which lowers the default boundary.
Optimism about transformative AI, backed by training investment, literally keeps the firm alive longer.
This creates a knife-edge for levered firms: they must invest early enough to capture market share and maintain enough training to sustain the H-regime option value, but not so aggressively that they risk default if the regime switch fails to materialize.

Third, the AI investment dilemma reveals an asymmetric cost of belief mismatches: the downside of overinvestment (excess leverage, misallocated training, default risk) exceeds the opportunity cost of underinvestment (delayed entry, missed revenue) for the same magnitude of belief error.
The training channel amplifies this asymmetry: an overly optimistic firm not only builds too much capacity but over-allocates to training, sacrificing the inference revenue it needs to survive the pre-switch period.
This asymmetry implies that the extreme capital commitments observed in practice can be rationalized only by genuinely optimistic beliefs, not by strategic posturing or agency problems.

The model necessarily abstracts from several dimensions of the full decision space that executives navigate. It does not capture talent constraints, safety and alignment objectives, geopolitical competition from actors like DeepSeek [@deepseek2025r1], or the ongoing debate about whether pre-training scaling has reached its limits [@sutskever2024scaling]. It treats the training fraction as fixed at investment time, whereas dynamic reallocation and inference-time scaling [@brown2024inference] create a richer optimization. And it does not address the \$600 billion revenue gap between AI infrastructure spending and current AI revenue [@cahn2024gap] that may discipline the investment race through financial markets before any technological resolution occurs. These omissions define the frontier for extending the framework developed here.

Several directions for future research are promising.
Dynamic learning models in which firms update their beliefs about $\lambda$ in response to AI capability milestones---such as the shift from pre-training to inference-time scaling, or algorithmic efficiency breakthroughs like DeepSeek's demonstration that frontier reasoning is achievable at dramatically lower cost---would endogenize the belief heterogeneity across firms.
Dynamic training-inference reallocation, allowing $\phi$ to respond to realized demand and capability milestones, would enrich the model's predictions about the time path of compute allocation.
Incorporating financial frictions---credit constraints, dynamic leverage adjustment, and debt renegotiation---would enrich the default mechanism.
A revealed beliefs methodology that inverts the structural model to extract implied AI timeline beliefs from observable investment decisions (using investment intensity and training fraction as identifying moments) is a natural empirical companion; the fragility of such an inversion to calibration assumptions---particularly H-regime volatility---suggests that formal identification and estimation require careful treatment beyond the scope of this paper.
